title: Controlled inputs

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Controlled inputs; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Controlled inputs
sources.claim: A controlled form field receives its current value from React state and reports edits through onChange.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Controlled inputs
sources.claim: A controlled form field receives its current value from React state and reports edits through onChange.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A controlled form field receives its current value from React state and reports edits through onChange.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An input uses value={name} and updates name from event.target.value with a persistent label. Your goal is to implement and test text, choice and checkbox controls without controlled/uncontrolled warnings. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State and events; html forms; useState and snapshots; html forms labels controls. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: A controlled form field receives its current value from React state and reports edits through onChange. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Every keystroke renders, nullish value transitions can switch control mode, and file inputs remain special. This draft therefore rejects: Providing an initial defaultValue makes an input controlled forever.

includes: Bind value or checked state to React while preserving labels, input semantics and responsive updates.
Implement and test text, choice and checkbox controls without controlled/uncontrolled warnings.
excludes: Every keystroke renders, nullish value transitions can switch control mode, and file inputs remain special.

outcomes: Implement and test text, choice and checkbox controls without controlled/uncontrolled warnings.

examples.title: Controlled inputs: concrete case
examples.situation: An input uses value={name} and updates name from event.target.value with a persistent label.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: An input uses value={name} and updates name from event.target.value with a persistent label.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A controlled input uses value plus an update handler; defaultValue initializes an uncontrolled input.
examples.result: The result follows because A controlled input uses value plus an update handler; defaultValue initializes an uncontrolled input.

examples.title: Controlled inputs: boundary case
examples.situation: Every keystroke renders, nullish value transitions can switch control mode, and file inputs remain special.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Every keystroke renders, nullish value transitions can switch control mode, and file inputs remain special.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Providing an initial defaultValue makes an input controlled forever.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: A controlled input uses value plus an update handler; defaultValue initializes an uncontrolled input.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Providing an initial defaultValue makes an input controlled forever.

limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.
